text: In this video, we will present the laparoscopic repair of left para duodenal hernia. Para duodenal hernia is a type of internal abdominal hernias and occurs when the small bowel herniates into the left or right para duodenal fossa.

The clinical manifestation can be ranged from recurrent abdominal pain attacks to acute intestinal obstruction with or without strangulation. Although para duodenal hernias have unique computed tomography appearance, the diagnosis of para duodenal hernias can be missed in emergency settings.
The surgeon reduces the herniated small bowel loops and closes the hernia orifice during the laparoscopic repair of a left para duodenal hernia.
In the majority of previous reports, open approach was used for hernia repair, however laparoscopic repair is also possible when the diagnosis is made preoperatively.

The presented case is a twenty-two year old male and admitted to the emergency department with abdominal pain and vomiting. The patient expressed several previous visits to emergency departments owing to similar complaints and no definite diagnosis was established.

The CT scan reveals a sac-like cluster of small bowel loops herniated into the left para duodenal fossa, located posteriorly to the transverse colon. The hernia sac shows signs of fluid accumulation and congestion.

During the surgery, the transverse mesocolon is retracted cordially, revealing the hernia orifice on the left side of the duodenal-jejunal junction. The incarcerated small bowel loops are visible within the hernia sac.

The surgeon successfully reduces all small bowel loops into the peritoneal cavity, reaching the area just after the fourth part of the duodenum. The visual inspection confirms no signs of small bowel ischemia.
The surgeon evaluates the hernia sac, observing a small amount of serohemorrhagic fluid. A non-absorbable suture is used with a laparoscopic suturing device to close the hernia orifice.
The surgeon uses the purse string method to close the hernia orifice, taking serosal bites from the jejunal segment to ensure complete closure.
The surgeon inspects the closure of the hernia orifice, ensuring it is secure before completing the operation.

After surgery, patient was discharged on post-operative third day without any complication and he has not experienced any complaints in two-year follow-up period. As a conclusion, laparoscopic surgery can be used as a feasible and safe approach in the treatment of left para-duodenal hernia.
